questions.question: 1. In a Fraunhofer diffraction experiment, what does the order (k) in the formula d sinθ = kλ refer to?

answers.a: The number of diffracting slits used
answers.b: The colour of the light source
answers.c: The type of material used for the aperture
answers.d: The location of a particular dark fringe relative to the central maximum

explanations.a: Number of slits is a parameter of diffraction gratings, not single-slit order.
explanations.b: Colour is related to wavelength, not to the order number.
explanations.c: Material of the aperture is irrelevant to fringe order.
explanations.d: Correct. k = 1, 2, 3, … denotes the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, … dark fringe (minimum) on either side of the central maximum. Learn more

questions.question: 2. A single slit diffracts white light. Which colour deviates the most from the straight-through (central) direction?

answers.d: All colours deviate by the same amount

explanations.a: Correct. From d sinθ = kλ, larger λ means larger θ for the same order. Red has the longest wavelength in the visible spectrum and therefore diffracts the most. Reference
explanations.b: Green has a medium wavelength and therefore an intermediate deviation.
explanations.c: Blue has a shorter wavelength than red and deviates less.
explanations.d: Different colours have different wavelengths and therefore different deviation angles.

questions.question: 3. Which parameter must be measured in the simulation to calculate the wavelength of the He–Ne laser in a single-slit diffraction setup?

answers.a: Distance between two consecutive bright fringes
answers.b: Position of the central maximum only
answers.c: Angle of observation (θ) at the first-order minimum
answers.d: Total intensity of the diffraction pattern

explanations.a: Distances between bright fringes are harder to measure precisely; minima positions are used.
explanations.b: The central maximum is at θ = 0° — it does not give wavelength information.
explanations.c: Correct. Measuring the angle θ at the k-th minimum and applying d sinθ = kλ gives the wavelength.
explanations.d: Total intensity is a photometric quantity and is not used in this formula.

questions.question: 4. If the distance between the slit and the screen (D) is doubled in a single-slit diffraction experiment (keeping d and λ constant), the physical fringe width on the screen will:

explanations.a: Fringe width increases with D, so halving D would give half the fringe width — not doubling.
explanations.b: Correct. Fringe width β = λD/d; doubling D doubles β.
explanations.c: Fringe width would be four times only if D were squared in the formula, which it is not.
explanations.d: Angular positions are fixed, but physical distances on the screen depend on D.

questions.question: 5. He–Ne laser light is used in diffraction experiments mainly because:

answers.a: It generates a large amount of heat
answers.b: It produces monochromatic and coherent light
answers.d: It produces a highly divergent beam

explanations.a: Heat is not a useful property for diffraction experiments.
explanations.b: Correct. The coherence and monochromaticity of He–Ne laser light produce sharp, well-defined diffraction minima.
explanations.c: A laser does not change its colour during propagation.
explanations.d: Lasers are highly collimated (low divergence), which is another reason they are preferred.

questions.question: 6. In a single-slit diffraction experiment, d = 0.1 mm and the first-order minimum is observed at θ = 0.363°. What is the wavelength of the laser?

explanations.b: λ = 532 nm would require a different slit width or angle.
explanations.c: Correct. λ = d sinθ = 0.1×10⁻³ × sin(0.363°) ≈ 6.33×10⁻⁷ m = 633 nm ≈ 632.8 nm (He–Ne laser).
explanations.d: 780 nm is near-infrared; the He–Ne laser emits in the red visible range.

questions.question: 7. In the intensity formula for single-slit diffraction I = I₀(sinα/α)², what is the intensity at the centre of the central maximum (θ = 0)?

explanations.a: Zero intensity would mean a dark centre, which contradicts observation.
explanations.b: The intensity at centre is not halved.
explanations.c: This value would apply at a specific off-axis point, not at the centre.
explanations.d: Correct. At θ = 0, α → 0 and sinα/α → 1, so I = I₀(1)² = I₀, the maximum intensity.

questions.question: 8. Which of the following correctly describes the secondary maxima in a single-slit diffraction pattern?

answers.a: They are brighter than the central maximum
answers.c: Their intensity decreases with increasing order
answers.d: Their width is twice the width of the central maximum

explanations.a: The central maximum is always the brightest region.
explanations.b: Secondary maxima have decreasing intensity as the order increases.
explanations.c: Correct. The intensity of successive secondary maxima decreases because the effective slit area contributing constructively reduces with each higher order.
explanations.d: Secondary maxima are half the width of the central maximum, not double.

questions.question: 9. In a single-slit diffraction experiment, which combination produces the sharpest (narrowest) central maximum?

explanations.a: Correct. Angular half-width = λ/d; to minimise it, use the smallest λ and the largest d (widest slit).
explanations.b: Narrow slit and long wavelength both increase the angular width — giving the broadest central maximum.
explanations.c: A narrow slit increases diffraction spreading; although short λ helps, the narrow slit effect dominates.
explanations.d: Long wavelength increases spreading even though a wide slit reduces it.

questions.question: 10. If the slit width in a single-slit diffraction experiment is made equal to the wavelength of light (d = λ), the first-order minimum will appear at:

answers.d: No minimum will be observed on the screen

explanations.b: sin45° ≈ 0.707 ≠ 1, so this is not the correct angle.
explanations.c: Correct. From d sinθ = kλ with k = 1 and d = λ: sinθ = 1, so θ = 90°. The minimum falls at the edge of the screen — in practice, the entire screen shows only the central maximum.
explanations.d: A minimum exists at θ = 90°, though it cannot be observed on a flat screen in front of the slit.
